DepartedPlayer.brief: Represents a player who has left a game or session, organized within a doubly linked list.
DepartedPlayer.details: The DepartedPlayer class models players who have departed from a game or session, storing their identifying name and linking to other departed players through 'next' and 'previous' pointers to form a doubly linked list. This structure allows efficient traversal and management of the list of departed players, supporting operations such as insertion, removal, and search. The class provides a default constructor for creating instances that can be initialized later, and a virtual destructor to ensure proper cleanup, especially when subclassed. Copy construction and assignment are disabled to maintain list integrity and prevent unintended duplication of player records. This class is integral to systems that track player departures, enabling features like historical record keeping, display, and analysis of departed players within the broader application architecture.
DepartedPlayer.notes: Implements a doubly linked list node pattern with private copy constructor and assignment operator to prevent copying, ensuring list integrity. The use of a virtual destructor allows safe inheritance if extended.
DepartedPlayer.rationale: This class exists to manage and organize records of players who have left the session, facilitating efficient list operations and ensuring proper resource management within the system's player tracking infrastructure.
DepartedPlayer.name: DepartedPlayer

name.brief: Stores the name of a departed player for identification and display purposes.
name.details: The `name` member holds the identifier of a player who has left the game, enabling operations such as comparison, display, and persistence. It is initialized when a player departs and is used across multiple functions to locate, remove, or present departed players. The variable leverages the custom `String` class, which offers case-insensitive comparison and advanced string manipulation, ensuring consistent and flexible handling of player names throughout the system.
name.notes: Utilizes the `String` class for case-insensitive operations, which helps prevent issues related to name casing inconsistencies.
name.rationale: This variable exists to uniquely identify departed players within the system, supporting features like searching, displaying, and saving the list of players who have left the game.
name.name: name

usages.act__info_8cc, void do_departed(Characte: Serves as a temporary buffer to hold each departed player's name for display purposes.

next.brief: Pointer to the next DepartedPlayer node in the linked list.
next.details: The 'next' pointer in the DepartedPlayer class connects individual departed player records, forming a singly linked list structure. It is initialized to nullptr and is assigned to point to subsequent nodes during list insertion or linking operations. This setup facilitates efficient traversal, insertion, and management of departed player data, enabling functionalities such as loading, saving, searching, and iterating through the list across various parts of the codebase.
next.notes: The 'next' pointer is typically used in loop constructs to traverse or modify the list, and proper management ensures no memory leaks or dangling pointers occur during list operations.
next.rationale: This variable exists to enable dynamic, flexible management of departed player records, supporting operations that require sequential access or modification of the list, such as adding new entries or searching for specific players.
next.name: next

usages.departed_8cc, void load_departed_list(): Used to link the newly created DepartedPlayer object to the list, setting the 'next' pointer of departed_list_head.
usages.departed_8cc, bool has_departed(const St: Used to traverse the linked list by updating 'iterator' to 'iterator->next' in each loop iteration.
usages.departed_8cc, void remove_departed(const: Accessed to traverse the linked list by moving to the next node during iteration.
usages.departed_8cc, void insert_departed(const: Used to traverse the linked list from the current iterator position to find the correct insertion point.
usages.departed_8cc, void save_departed_list(): Used to traverse the linked list of departed players in a loop until reaching the tail.
usages.act__info_8cc, void do_departed(Characte: Used to iterate through the linked list of departed players, advancing to the next node in each loop iteration.

previous.brief: Pointer to the previous DepartedPlayer in the doubly linked list structure.
previous.details: The 'previous' member holds a reference to the DepartedPlayer instance that comes before the current node within the doubly linked list managing departed players. It is initialized to nullptr upon node creation, signifying that the node has no predecessor initially. This pointer is crucial for list operations such as insertion, removal, and traversal, enabling backward navigation and efficient re-linking of nodes. It is typically assigned during list modifications like loading, inserting, or deleting nodes to preserve the list's integrity and facilitate navigation through the sequence.
previous.notes: This pointer is often set during list construction or re-linking processes, ensuring consistent backward links. Proper management of 'previous' is essential to avoid dangling pointers or list corruption.
previous.rationale: The 'previous' pointer supports efficient backward traversal and simplifies list re-linking operations, maintaining the integrity of the doubly linked list when players are added or removed.
previous.name: previous

usages.departed_8cc, void load_departed_list(): Used to link the newly created DepartedPlayer object to the list, setting the 'previous' pointer of departed_list_tail.
usages.departed_8cc, void remove_departed(const: Accessed to re-link the list when removing a node, specifically to connect the previous node to the next node after deletion.
usages.departed_8cc, void insert_departed(const: Used to set the previous pointer of the new DepartedPlayer and to navigate backwards in the list when inserting at the end.

DepartedPlayer::DepartedPlayer().brief: Constructs a new DepartedPlayer instance without initializing its members.
DepartedPlayer::DepartedPlayer().details: This default constructor creates an instance of the DepartedPlayer class without performing any explicit initialization of its member variables. It relies on default member initializations or uninitialized state, making it suitable for scenarios where the object will be configured later. This approach allows for flexible object creation, especially when the specific initialization data is not available at construction time.
DepartedPlayer::DepartedPlayer().returns: This constructor does not return a value.
DepartedPlayer::DepartedPlayer().notes: Since this constructor does not initialize member variables, users should ensure proper initialization before using the object to avoid undefined behavior.
DepartedPlayer::DepartedPlayer().rationale: This constructor is used when creating DepartedPlayer objects that will be set up later, such as during deserialization or when default-constructed objects are needed before assigning meaningful data.
DepartedPlayer::DepartedPlayer().name: DepartedPlayer

virtual DepartedPlayer::~DepartedPlayer(.brief: Virtual destructor for the DepartedPlayer class to ensure proper cleanup of resources.
virtual DepartedPlayer::~DepartedPlayer(.details: This destructor is declared as virtual to guarantee that when a DepartedPlayer object or a derived class instance is deleted through a base class pointer, the appropriate destructor sequence is invoked. Although it is defaulted and does not contain custom cleanup code, its virtual nature is crucial for correct resource management in polymorphic scenarios. The destructor relies on automatic destruction of member variables and base classes, ensuring that all associated resources are released appropriately during object destruction.
virtual DepartedPlayer::~DepartedPlayer(.returns: This function does not return a value.
virtual DepartedPlayer::~DepartedPlayer(.notes: Having a virtual destructor is essential in classes intended for inheritance to prevent resource leaks and undefined behavior during object cleanup.
virtual DepartedPlayer::~DepartedPlayer(.rationale: This destructor is used when a DepartedPlayer object is destroyed, especially in contexts involving inheritance, to ensure that all resources are properly released and that derived class destructors are called correctly.
virtual DepartedPlayer::~DepartedPlayer(.name: ~DepartedPlayer

DepartedPlayer & DepartedPlayer::operato.details: This copy assignment operator allows one DepartedPlayer object to be assigned the state of another DepartedPlayer object. It ensures that the current object becomes a copy of the provided object, handling any necessary cleanup of existing resources and copying of new resources.

params.The: DepartedPlayer object whose state is to be copied.
